title: WSmart+ Route — Adaptive Routing Policies for Smart Waste Collection
subtitle: Simulation Study of Selection Strategies, Route Constructors and Improvers
author: Afonso Fernandes
agenda: The Vehicle Routing Problem with Profits (VRPP)
Route Improvers: Classical Local Search vs Fast-TSP

vrpp.title: The Problem: Vehicle Routing Problem with Profits (VRPP)
equation: $\max_{y,\,x}\ \ \sum_{i \in V} p_i\, y_i\ -\ c \sum_{(i,j) \in A} d_{ij}\, x_{ij}$
$\mathrm{s.t.}\ \ \sum_{i \in V} q_i\, y_i \leq Q,\quad x\ \mathrm{routes\ the\ selected}\ y$
vrpp.caption: Objective: maximise collected profit minus travel cost, choosing which nodes y to visit and how x routes them, subject to the vehicle capacity Q.
bullets: Unlike the classical VRP, only a profitable subset of nodes is visited: profit p_i (waste kg) vs travel cost c·d_ij
In waste collection bins fill stochastically day by day — skipping a bin saves distance but risks overflow
Two conflicting KPIs: bin overflows (service quality, ↓) and kg/km route efficiency (cost, ↑)
Multi-day sequential decision problem over scenarios: RM-100, RM-170, FFZ-350 × {Empirical, Gamma-3} × {30, 90} days

simulator.title: Routing Simulator Philosophy — Modular Policy Pipeline
simulator.caption: Every daily routing policy decomposes into a mandatory bin-selection strategy, a route constructor, an optional acceptance criterion and an optional route improver.
bullets: Every daily routing policy is a composition of interchangeable stages, benchmarked under identical conditions
The simulator replays multi-day scenarios (region × graph size × waste distribution), logging overflows, kg, km, kg/km and profit for every policy combination

strategies.title: Mandatory Node Selection Strategies
strategies.caption: The three mandatory selection rules — Look-Ahead (LA) forecasts fill level f_i at t+δ, Last-Minute (LM) reacts once f_i crosses a critical fill threshold CF, and Service-Level (SL) bounds each bin's overflow probability by a target confidence α.
bullets: Look-Ahead anticipates predicted overflows; Last-Minute waits for a critical fill threshold; Service-Level bounds each bin's overflow probability
Empirical trade-off: LM dominates efficiency (kg/km), SL dominates overflow prevention, LA sits in between
The choice spans the service-quality vs cost spectrum — an operational decision, not an algorithmic one

exact.title: Exact Solvers: BPC & SWC-TCF
exact.caption: Set-partitioning master problem: choose a minimum-cost combination of routes r ∈ Ω (weight λ_r) such that every node i is covered exactly once (a_ir = 1 iff route r visits i).
bullets: Branch-Price-and-Cut (BPC): column generation over the route set Ω with cutting planes — proven optimality (BHV2000 lineage)
Pricing subproblem: elementary shortest path with resource constraints, accelerated by ng-route relaxation and limited-memory cuts
SWC-TCF: exact set-partitioning over Clarke-Wright-style savings columns with traffic-congestion-adjusted costs
Both provide optimality reference points; exponential worst-case runtime limits them to the daily subproblem sizes

metaheuristics.title: Meta-Heuristics & Hyper-Heuristics — Overarching Philosophy
metaheuristics.caption: A meta-heuristic maps the incumbent x_t to a new candidate via operator A parameterised by θ_t, updated from feedback; a hyper-heuristic instead searches over low-level heuristics h ∈ H, picking the one with highest estimated value Q_t(h) at each step.
bullets: Heuristic search trades a small optimality gap for polynomial runtime — essential beyond the exact solvers' size limits
Meta-heuristics balance exploration (diversify, escape local optima) and exploitation (intensify, polish good solutions)
Hyper-heuristics search the space of heuristics itself, learning which low-level operator to apply next (e.g. ACO-HH)
Three families studied: evolutionary (HGS), swarm intelligence (PSOMA, ACO-HH), neighborhood search (SANS, PG-CLNS)

evolutionary.title: Evolutionary Algorithms — HGS (Hybrid Genetic Search)
evolutionary.caption: Biased fitness function used to rank current solutions by both quality and diversity. n_pop is the current population size and n_elite the number of elite (best-cost) individuals; individuals outside the elite are increasingly rewarded for contributing diversity.
bullets: Population-based: parents recombine via ordered crossover; every offspring is 'educated' by aggressive local search
Biased fitness (above) blends solution quality with diversity contribution — prevents premature convergence
Infeasible individuals survive in a penalised sub-population, letting the search cross infeasible regions
State-of-the-art on classical CVRP; strong efficiency performer here when paired with the CLS improver

swarm.title: Swarm Intelligence — PSOMA & ACO-HH
equation: $\mathbf{PSO:}\ \ v_i^{t+1} = \omega\, v_i^t + c_1 r_1 \left(p_i - x_i^t\right) + c_2 r_2 \left(g - x_i^t\right)$
swarm.caption: PSO velocity update: particle i moves toward its personal best p_i and the global best g under inertia ω and acceleration coefficients c1, c2. ACO pheromone update: pheromone τ_h on heuristic h decays by evaporation rate ρ and is reinforced by the deposits Δτ_h of the k ants that used it.
bullets: Many simple agents share information; global order emerges from local interactions
PSOMA: particles attracted by personal/global bests + a memetic local-search refinement phase — consistent best-overflow performer in the empirical scenarios
ACO-HH: pheromones are laid over low-level heuristics (h) rather than arcs — the colony learns the best sequence of constructive operators
ACO-HH is the most robust all-rounder: Pareto-optimal in multiple scenarios (zero-overflow SL2 configs at FFZ-350)

neighborhood.title: Neighborhood Search — SANS & PG-CLNS
neighborhood.caption: Simulated annealing accepts a worsening move with probability e^(−Δ/T_t), cooling temperature T by factor γ each iteration. PG-CLNS learns its destroy/repair operator choice o via the policy-gradient estimator ∇_θJ, using state s (fill levels, geography) and observed reward R.
bullets: Trajectory-based: iteratively perturb the incumbent through neighbourhood moves
SANS: simulated annealing accepts worsening moves with probability e^(−Δ/T); cooling shifts exploration → exploitation
PG-CLNS: destroy-and-repair large-neighborhood search whose operator selection is learned online via policy gradients, adapting to instance context (fill levels, geography)
PG-CLNS is a standout overflow performer: SL1 + PG-CLNS reaches the Pareto front with ~1 overflow / 30 days

improvers.title: Route Improvers: Classical Local Search vs Fast-TSP
improvers.caption: 2-opt accepts an edge swap whenever the resulting distance change Δ is negative. Fast-TSP reorders each route's visiting sequence π to minimise total distance — solved exactly for small routes and via Stochastic Local Search (SLS) once N exceeds 20 stops.
bullets: Optional, constructor-agnostic post-optimisation of each constructed route
CLS: exhaustive intra/inter-route neighbourhoods (2-opt, or-opt, swap, relocate) to a local optimum — best kg/km polish
Fast-TSP: reorders each route as a TSP — exact for small routes, Stochastic Local Search (SLS) under the hood for graphs with more than 20 nodes
Improver choice is a second-order effect: selection strategy and constructor dominate the KPI outcomes

conclusion.title: Conclusions, Limitations & Future Work
conclusion.caption: Overlaid radar chart comparing key route constructors across overflow, efficiency, distance and profit axes — outer position indicates better performance on that axis.
bullets: The selection strategy dominates the overflow/efficiency trade-off: SL prevents overflows (SL2 + ACO_HH reached 0 at FFZ-350), LM (CF90) maximises kg/km (~12), LA balances
BPC and PG-CLNS are the most frequent Pareto-front constructors; ACO-HH is the most robust across scenarios and horizons
Patterns are stable from 30 to 90 days; overflow pressure grows with the horizon while kg/km stays consistent
Limitations: single vehicle type/depot per region; waste models calibrated on limited data; 90-day runs only cover the 30-day Pareto-front policies
Future work: richer acceptance criteria, neural constructors in the same pipeline, multi-vehicle fleets, intra-day re-routing, CO₂-aware objectives

qa.title: Thank You — Questions & Answers
bullets: Thank you for your attention!
Time for questions

pareto.title: Results — Pareto Front (Overflows vs kg/km)
pareto.note: Symlog X axis (overflows). Colour = selection variant, shape = scenario, dashed lines = Pareto fronts (one colour per scenario).

kpi.title: Results — Summary KPI Analysis by Selection Strategy
kpi.note: Mean ± min/max range across route constructors; colours discriminate the mandatory selection strategies.

scenario_heatmaps.title: Results — Per-Scenario Heatmaps (Constructors × Strategy·Improver)
scenario_heatmaps.note: 30-day runs. One panel per scenario; route constructors on the rows, selection × improver combinations on the columns.

heatmaps.title: Results — Overflow & kg/km Heatmaps, 90 Days (Policies × Scenarios)
heatmaps.note: 90-day runs (Pareto-front policies only — fewer rows, easier to read). Rows = policy configurations; columns = scenarios.

improver_bubble.title: Results — Route Improver Comparison
improver_bubble.note: Each bubble = (improver, scenario) averaged over strategies and constructors — FTSP vs CLS contrasted directly.
